news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights renewed advocacy for albinism rights, calls for prioritizing human rights in Bangladesh, and a strengthened global commitment to justice in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées enregistrées aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme mettent en lumière un regain d’engagement en faveur des droits des personnes atteintes d’albinisme, des appels à donner la priorité aux droits de l’homme au Bangladesh, ainsi qu’un engagement mondial renforcé en faveur de la justice en République démocratique du Congo.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve una renovada defensa de los derechos de las personas con albinismo, los llamamientos a dar prioridad a los derechos humanos en Bangladés y un compromiso mundial más firme con la justicia en la República Democrática del Congo.

articles.title: Reimagining Albinism Rights Advocacy
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Human Rights Watch Director of the Disability Rights Division, Elizabeth Kamundia (left), and Marco Bristo Fellow for Courageous Leadership in Disability Rights, Hilda Macheso (center), interview people in Malawi, October 2025.
                    © 2025 Samer Muscati/Human Rights Watch 
          
    


  
It is a hot afternoon in a rural district in Malawi. People gather around a tent set up at the Local Trading Center, eager to witness a community outreach program that aims to combat rising violence against people with albinism.The organizers passionately encourage greater understanding of albinism and the protection of the rights of people with albinism. They ask questions to ensure the listeners have comprehended what has been discussed. At the appointed time, the advocates depart, pleased with the community’s feedback during this short session. “This outreach has been a success,” they say.While these sessions raise awareness, they often do not actively engage participants in challenging the underlying stigma. They’re mostly passive, so attendees may remember the information briefly, but there’s little follow-up or practical engagement to transform attitudes or behaviors in the long term. Tracking lasting change or impact is difficult.As a disability rights advocate, I have seen how unsustainable this approach can be. Also, as a theater practitioner, I aim to combine what I learned in class with my advocacy work. In my efforts to help the community understand albinism, I have utilized an applied theater technique called forum theater.I create short plays based on real-life injustices faced by people with albinism, illustrating how these barriers deprive both individuals and their communities of their full participation. I offer the audience a glimpse into our lived experiences and let them feel what we feel.Applied theater approaches usually include follow-up or visits to address any emerging issues and reinforce learning. This makes applied theater more effective for sustaining behavioral or attitudinal change.Forum theater also invites community members to literally step into the shoes of the characters during the play to suggest and enact their own solutions. This co-creation of ideas empowers the community to address injustices and actively support people with albinism.Furthermore, this approach enables me to record the community’s proposed solutions and turn them into tangible commitments. These then serve as a foundation for tracking progress to ensure that the rights of people with albinism are actively protected through the very actions the community itself suggests.I believe that, to be fully effective and sustainable, advocacy needs to move beyond just performance. It has to become a shared act of critical reflection, one that is sustainable and bears fruit long after the advocacy activities have ended.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Human Rights Watch Director of the Disability Rights Division, Elizabeth Kamundia (left), and Marco Bristo Fellow for Courageous Leadership in Disability Rights, Hilda Macheso (center), interview people in Malawi, October 2025. © 2025 Samer Muscati/Human Rights Watch

It is a hot afternoon in a rural district in Malawi. People gather around a tent set up at the Local Trading Center, eager to witness a community outreach program that aims to combat rising violence against people with albinism.

The organizers passionately encourage greater understanding of albinism and the protection of the rights of people with albinism. They ask questions to ensure the listeners have comprehended what has been discussed. At the appointed time, the advocates depart, pleased with the community’s feedback during this short session. “This outreach has been a success,” they say.

While these sessions raise awareness, they often do not actively engage participants in challenging the underlying stigma. They’re mostly passive, so attendees may remember the information briefly, but there’s little follow-up or practical engagement to transform attitudes or behaviors in the long term. Tracking lasting change or impact is difficult.

As a disability rights advocate, I have seen how unsustainable this approach can be. Also, as a theater practitioner, I aim to combine what I learned in class with my advocacy work. In my efforts to help the community understand albinism, I have utilized an applied theater technique called forum theater.

I create short plays based on real-life injustices faced by people with albinism, illustrating how these barriers deprive both individuals and their communities of their full participation. I offer the audience a glimpse into our lived experiences and let them feel what we feel.

Applied theater approaches usually include follow-up or visits to address any emerging issues and reinforce learning. This makes applied theater more effective for sustaining behavioral or attitudinal change.

Forum theater also invites community members to literally step into the shoes of the characters during the play to suggest and enact their own solutions. This co-creation of ideas empowers the community to address injustices and actively support people with albinism.

Furthermore, this approach enables me to record the community’s proposed solutions and turn them into tangible commitments. These then serve as a foundation for tracking progress to ensure that the rights of people with albinism are actively protected through the very actions the community itself suggests.

I believe that, to be fully effective and sustainable, advocacy needs to move beyond just performance. It has to become a shared act of critical reflection, one that is sustainable and bears fruit long after the advocacy activities have ended.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful and innovative approach to disability rights advocacy for people with albinism in Malawi, using applied forum theater to engage communities actively, challenge stigma, and create sustainable behavioral change. This approach empowers communities to co-create solutions and track progress, representing a significant positive impact on human rights and social inclusion.
articles.personality_title: Using Forum Theater to Support People with Albinism in Malawi
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Malawi, people with albinism often face violence and stigma. Traditional awareness programs try to educate communities but usually do not change deep attitudes or behaviors over time.

**What happened** – A disability rights advocate in Malawi started using forum theater to help communities understand albinism better. This method involves acting out real-life stories about the challenges people with albinism face. Audience members can join the play, suggest solutions, and act them out, making the experience interactive and personal.

**Impact** – Unlike regular talks, forum theater helps people feel the struggles of those with albinism and think actively about how to solve problems. It encourages the whole community to participate and creates real commitments to protect rights. This approach is more likely to lead to lasting changes in how people treat those with albinism.

**What's next step** – The advocate plans to keep using forum theater in different communities and follow up on the promises made during the plays. This will help track progress and make sure the rights of people with albinism continue to improve.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Forum theater is a new way to fight stigma against people with albinism by involving communities in acting out problems and creating real solutions together.
articles.personality_title_fr: Utiliser le théâtre forum pour soutenir les personnes atteintes d’albinisme au Malawi
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Malawi, les personnes atteintes d’albinisme subissent souvent des violences et des préjugés. Les programmes d’information traditionnels essaient d’éduquer les communautés, mais ne changent généralement pas les attitudes ou comportements profonds sur le long terme.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une militante des droits des personnes handicapées au Malawi a commencé à utiliser le théâtre forum pour mieux faire comprendre l’albinisme aux communautés. Cette méthode consiste à jouer des histoires vraies sur les difficultés rencontrées par les personnes atteintes d’albinisme. Les spectateurs peuvent participer à la pièce, proposer des solutions et les jouer, rendant l’expérience interactive et personnelle.

**Impact** – Contrairement aux simples discours, le théâtre forum aide les gens à ressentir les difficultés des personnes atteintes d’albinisme et à réfléchir activement aux solutions. Il encourage toute la communauté à participer et crée de véritables engagements pour protéger les droits. Cette approche est plus susceptible de provoquer des changements durables dans le traitement des personnes atteintes d’albinisme.

**Prochaine étape** – La militante prévoit de continuer à utiliser le théâtre forum dans différentes communautés et de faire un suivi des promesses faites pendant les pièces. Cela aidera à suivre les progrès et à garantir que les droits des personnes atteintes d’albinisme s’améliorent.

**Phrase clé** – Le théâtre forum est une nouvelle façon de lutter contre la stigmatisation des personnes atteintes d’albinisme en impliquant les communautés à jouer les problèmes et à créer ensemble de vraies solutions.
articles.personality_title_es: Uso del teatro foro para apoyar a personas con albinismo en Malawi
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Malawi, las personas con albinismo a menudo enfrentan violencia y estigma. Los programas tradicionales de concientización intentan educar a las comunidades, pero generalmente no cambian las actitudes o comportamientos profundos a largo plazo.

**Qué pasó** – Una defensora de los derechos de las personas con discapacidad en Malawi comenzó a usar el teatro foro para ayudar a las comunidades a entender mejor el albinismo. Este método consiste en representar historias reales sobre los desafíos que enfrentan las personas con albinismo. Los espectadores pueden participar en la obra, sugerir soluciones y representarlas, haciendo la experiencia interactiva y personal.

**Impacto** – A diferencia de las charlas normales, el teatro foro ayuda a las personas a sentir las dificultades de quienes tienen albinismo y a pensar activamente en cómo resolver los problemas. Anima a toda la comunidad a participar y crea compromisos reales para proteger los derechos. Este enfoque es más probable que produzca cambios duraderos en el trato hacia las personas con albinismo.

**Próximo paso** – La defensora planea seguir usando el teatro foro en diferentes comunidades y hacer un seguimiento de las promesas hechas durante las obras. Esto ayudará a monitorear el progreso y asegurar que los derechos de las personas con albinismo sigan mejorando.

**Frase clave** – El teatro foro es una nueva forma de combatir el estigma contra las personas con albinismo al involucrar a las comunidades en actuar los problemas y crear soluciones reales juntos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Malawian village scene at a trading center, where a large, open tent shelters a diverse group of abstract, glowing silhouettes gathered attentively around an empty stage with symbolic white masks representing people with albinism, while nearby, shadowy figures step forward from the audience to join the stage, illustrating community members actively engaging and co-creating solutions through a forum theater performance, all rendered in natural, earthy tones.

articles.title: Bangladesh: New Government Should Prioritize Human Rights
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Tarique Rahman takes the oath of office as Prime Minister of Bangladesh at the National Parliament in Dhaka, Bangladesh, February 17, 2026.
                    © AP Photo/Mahmud Hossain Opu
          
    


  
(London) – Bangladesh’s recently elected prime minister, Tarique Rahman, and his Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) government face many urgent challenges but can use this opportunity to bring lasting protections of human rights, nine rights groups wrote in a letter to Rahman published today.Prime Minister Rahman came to office following a landslide election victory in February 2026. The election was conducted by an interim government that had replaced the increasingly abusive 15-year rule of the former prime minister, Sheikh Hasina, who was toppled by mass protests in 2024. While the widespread rights violations including enforced disappearances and extra-judicial killings under Hasina’s rule ended, the interim government continued to arbitrarily detain political opponents, and was unable to end mob violence against journalists, religious minorities, and cultural centers.“Tarique Rahman has been given a wide mandate to bring change, including by many Bangladeshis who risked their lives to overthrow an autocratic government,” said Meenakshi Ganguly, deputy Asia director at Human Rights Watch. “Success will require meaningful reform to ensure that independent institutions are capable of delivering accountability and upholding the rule of law, and real commitment to upholding rights such as freedom of religion and expression.”Among the priorities highlighted in the letter are ending arbitrary detention, holding those responsible for past violations accountable, abolishing the abusive Rapid Action Battalion, and protecting ethnic and religious minorities. The groups also said that the government should protect the rights of over a million Rohingya refugees currently in Bangladesh and establish a strong and independent National Human Rights Commission. The groups made specific recommendations in their letter for policy measures and legislative steps.During the election campaign, the BNP made numerous commitments to safeguard rights, including economic rights, by increasing the resources available for health, education, environmental protections, and social security.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Tarique Rahman takes the oath of office as Prime Minister of Bangladesh at the National Parliament in Dhaka, Bangladesh, February 17, 2026. © AP Photo/Mahmud Hossain Opu

(London) – Bangladesh’s recently elected prime minister, Tarique Rahman, and his Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) government face many urgent challenges but can use this opportunity to bring lasting protections of human rights, nine rights groups wrote in a letter to Rahman published today.

Prime Minister Rahman came to office following a landslide election victory in February 2026. The election was conducted by an interim government that had replaced the increasingly abusive 15-year rule of the former prime minister, Sheikh Hasina, who was toppled by mass protests in 2024. While the widespread rights violations including enforced disappearances and extra-judicial killings under Hasina’s rule ended, the interim government continued to arbitrarily detain political opponents, and was unable to end mob violence against journalists, religious minorities, and cultural centers.

“Tarique Rahman has been given a wide mandate to bring change, including by many Bangladeshis who risked their lives to overthrow an autocratic government,” said Meenakshi Ganguly, deputy Asia director at Human Rights Watch. “Success will require meaningful reform to ensure that independent institutions are capable of delivering accountability and upholding the rule of law, and real commitment to upholding rights such as freedom of religion and expression.”

Among the priorities highlighted in the letter are ending arbitrary detention, holding those responsible for past violations accountable, abolishing the abusive Rapid Action Battalion, and protecting ethnic and religious minorities. The groups also said that the government should protect the rights of over a million Rohingya refugees currently in Bangladesh and establish a strong and independent National Human Rights Commission. The groups made specific recommendations in their letter for policy measures and legislative steps.

During the election campaign, the BNP made numerous commitments to safeguard rights, including economic rights, by increasing the resources available for health, education, environmental protections, and social security.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant political change in Bangladesh with a new government that has the opportunity and mandate to implement meaningful human rights reforms. This includes ending arbitrary detentions, protecting minorities and refugees, and establishing independent institutions for accountability. The scope is broad, affecting millions in Bangladesh and Rohingya refugees, and the article provides sufficient context and specific priorities, making it an inspiring development for human rights.
articles.personality_title: Bangladesh’s new government vows to strengthen human rights protections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Bangladesh has experienced political change after mass protests ended the 15-year rule of former Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina, whose government was criticized for serious human rights abuses. An interim government took over but still struggled with rights issues.

**What happened** – In February 2026, Tarique Rahman and his Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) won a large election victory. Rights groups have urged Rahman’s new government to focus on protecting human rights, including stopping arbitrary arrests, ending violence against minorities, and supporting over a million Rohingya refugees living in Bangladesh.

**Impact** – This is an important moment because Rahman’s government has a strong mandate from voters who demanded change. The call to create independent institutions and hold past abusers accountable could help build trust and fairness in Bangladesh’s society. Protecting minorities and refugees addresses urgent humanitarian needs affecting millions.

**What’s next step** – The government is expected to take concrete actions like abolishing abusive security forces, establishing an independent human rights commission, and improving laws to protect freedoms such as religion and expression. Success depends on following through with these reforms and increasing support for health, education, and social security.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Bangladesh’s new leadership has a clear chance to improve human rights and justice for all its people and refugees by carrying out promised reforms.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le nouveau gouvernement du Bangladesh s'engage à renforcer la protection des droits humains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Bangladesh a connu un changement politique après que des manifestations de masse ont mis fin aux 15 ans de pouvoir de l’ancienne Première ministre Sheikh Hasina, dont le gouvernement était critiqué pour de graves violations des droits humains. Un gouvernement intérimaire a pris le relais, mais a continué à rencontrer des problèmes de droits.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En février 2026, Tarique Rahman et son parti, le Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP), ont remporté une large victoire électorale. Des groupes de défense des droits ont exhorté le nouveau gouvernement à se concentrer sur la protection des droits humains, notamment en mettant fin aux arrestations arbitraires, à la violence contre les minorités, et en soutenant plus d’un million de réfugiés rohingyas vivant au Bangladesh.

**Impact** – Ce moment est important car le gouvernement de Rahman a un fort mandat des électeurs qui réclamaient du changement. La création d’institutions indépendantes et la responsabilité des auteurs d’abus passés pourraient renforcer la confiance et la justice dans la société bangladaise. La protection des minorités et des réfugiés répond à des besoins humanitaires urgents qui touchent des millions de personnes.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement devrait prendre des mesures concrètes comme abolir les forces de sécurité abusives, établir une commission indépendante des droits humains, et améliorer les lois pour protéger les libertés, notamment la religion et l’expression. Le succès dépendra de la mise en œuvre de ces réformes et de l’augmentation du soutien à la santé, l’éducation et la sécurité sociale.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La nouvelle direction du Bangladesh a une réelle opportunité d’améliorer les droits humains et la justice pour tous ses habitants et réfugiés en réalisant les réformes promises.
articles.personality_title_es: El nuevo gobierno de Bangladesh se compromete a fortalecer la protección de los derechos humanos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Bangladesh ha vivido un cambio político tras protestas masivas que terminaron con los 15 años de gobierno de la ex primera ministra Sheikh Hasina, cuyo gobierno fue criticado por graves violaciones de derechos humanos. Un gobierno interino tomó el mando pero continuó enfrentando problemas de derechos.

**Qué pasó** – En febrero de 2026, Tarique Rahman y su partido, el Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP), ganaron una gran victoria electoral. Grupos de derechos humanos han pedido al nuevo gobierno que se enfoque en proteger los derechos, incluyendo el fin de detenciones arbitrarias, la violencia contra minorías y el apoyo a más de un millón de refugiados rohingya que viven en Bangladesh.

**Impacto** – Este es un momento importante porque el gobierno de Rahman tiene un fuerte mandato de votantes que pedían cambios. Crear instituciones independientes y responsabilizar a los abusadores pasados puede ayudar a generar confianza y justicia en la sociedad bangladesí. Proteger a minorías y refugiados atiende necesidades humanitarias urgentes que afectan a millones.

**Próximos pasos** – Se espera que el gobierno tome medidas concretas como abolir fuerzas de seguridad abusivas, establecer una comisión independiente de derechos humanos y mejorar leyes para proteger libertades como la religión y la expresión. El éxito dependerá de implementar estas reformas y aumentar el apoyo a la salud, educación y seguridad social.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo liderazgo de Bangladesh tiene una oportunidad clara para mejorar los derechos humanos y la justicia para todas las personas y refugiados mediante las reformas prometidas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vibrant, open parliamentary chamber stylized as a blossoming lotus flower with petals symbolizing justice scales, a dove carrying an olive branch soaring above, and diverse hands releasing colorful lanterns into a clear sky, all rendered in warm, natural tones to represent human rights, peaceful governance, and hope for Bangladesh’s new era.

articles.title: François Kaserake Kamate on global complicity and the fight for the DRC
articles.summary: "I was of violence, war and the cycle of social injustices. I wanted to follow a non-violent path because I believed in peaceful strategies to change the paradigm."
articles.content:
“If we remain silent, Congo will soon disappear from the map,” said François Kaserake Kamate in an interview with Global Voices. For Kamate, a climate and human rights activist from the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), silence means the erasure of communities, livelihoods, and human dignity in a conflict the world has largely chosen to ignore. For 13 years, Kamate has been involved in non-violent movements for justice and accountability, often at great personal risk.

The Congo is one of the most resource-rich countries on Earth, yet its people remain among the poorest. For Kamate, this paradox lies at the heart of the conflict: “Our natural resources have turned into a curse.” Minerals essential to global supply chains, used in phones, electric cars, and batteries, drive armed violence rather than development. This “resource curse” is sustained by a devastating loop of corruption, colonial-era ethnic divisions, and normalizing violence and resource exploitation, including illegal mining, massacres, and state repression.

Non-violence in a militarized reality

In this environment of violence, activism is not a common path to follow in the DRC. Kamate explained:

People consider activists like us as useless and misguided. We walk several kilometers on foot with simple messages, and people do not understand why we are trying to solve problems we are not responsible for.

With the immediate threat of violence, child labor, and corruption, many young people of Kamate’s generation joined the militias to protect their resources, land, and families. He, however, chose a different path. He said:

I was of violence, war, and the cycle of social injustices. I wanted to follow a non-violent path because I believed in peaceful strategies to change the paradigm.

Kamate added:

But in the DRC, activism is treated as a threat. As soon as we tell them the truth, demand our rights, they see us as enemies. It really surprised me that the national security forces decided to kill our comrades during our peaceful protests.

This repression creates a widespread fear among civilians. Kamate explained: “Many times, when we organize our actions, people fear joining because they might lose their homes, their jobs, get arrested, tortured, or even lose their lives.”

Over the years, Kamate has been arrested countless times, had to relocate to Kinshasa, and even lost his job as an educator.

Three pillars of violence

Such intimidation tactics are part of a broader strategy used by those in power to maintain control. Kamate describes ignorance, poverty, and corruption as the three pillars that sustain politicians and ongoing violence. He explained:

They want people to be ignorant of their rights, too poor to think big, and therefore easier to corrupt.

Under these conditions, survival becomes entangled with political obedience. Many civilians accept payments to support political agendas, protests, or even speak out against activism, out of economic desperation, strengthening a system that thrives on dependence. These internal pillars are reinforced by external actors whose interests benefit from instability rather than change.

The international community and global responsibility

Despite decades of high-level NGO presence, awareness alone has not translated into meaningful change. While it’s been widely documented that militias and state forces abuse civilian rights, that armed groups operate in the interests of multinational companies, and that neighboring countries sponsor and support them to gain access to mineral wealth, NGOs or the international community often struggle to address root causes and structural violence. Kamate explained:

When projects are implemented by international organizations, they do not consider the values, local voices, or communities. That’s why it is very hard and difficult to get positive results, and most of these projects do not succeed.

This disconnection reflects a broader pattern in development and aid practices, known as “white saviorism,” where external solutions and interests overshadow local needs, knowledge, and agency.

Kamate describes this as performative condemnation: “They condemn them only on social media, radio, and television, but they do not stop this. They do not listen to us.”

This gap between knowledge and action allows exploitation and violence to persist within the global economic system and value chains. The minerals extracted are essential to phones, electric vehicles, and other everyday technologies, linking international consumers and demand directly to the realities on the ground in eastern Congo.

Hope as resistance

Responsibilities for the ongoing crisis are shared across political institutions, corporations, and global markets. Kamate challenges the idea of individual powerlessness: “Everybody has a role. If everyone participates, hand in hand, we can finally get what we are fighting for.”

Yet, decades of violence and repression have left many people unwilling or unable to speak out. Kamate’s activism focuses on rebuilding solidarity among those who have suffered. He works with women who have lost their children and families, encouraging them to believe that change remains possible through non-violent and peaceful methods.

In his interview with Global Voices, he repeatedly returned to the importance of hope. He said:

This is not the end of life. Even though the situation is getting worse, we must have hope that tomorrow will be better than today.

For Kamate, the urgency of the situation leaves no room for silence: “If I keep quiet, Congo in twenty years will disappear completely from the map. If I do not act now, then when? If not me, then who?”

His voice is one of many that need to be heard. There is a lack of space at both the international and national levels for young activists to express their demands, needs, and agendas. Kamate emphasizes that: “If we have those spaces, I think that our voices will be heard because young people are not only the future of society, as most people say. We are the present and the future at the same time.”

Kamate concluded:
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring non-violent activism of François Kaserake Kamate in the DRC, focusing on human rights and justice amidst ongoing conflict and exploitation. It emphasizes hope, peaceful resistance, and the call for global responsibility, which has broad significance for many affected by systemic violence and resource exploitation. The story provides substantial context about the challenges and the activist's efforts, making it a meaningful and uplifting narrative of resilience and advocacy.
articles.personality_title: Activist François Kamate fights peacefully for justice in Congo amid conflict
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is rich in natural resources but suffers from ongoing violence, corruption, and poverty. These problems have lasted for many years, making life difficult for the people who live there. Many groups, including armed militias, fight over control of valuable minerals used in phones and electric cars.

**What happened** – François Kaserake Kamate, a climate and human rights activist from eastern DRC, has spent 13 years leading peaceful protests and non-violent movements. Despite facing arrests, threats, and losing his job, he continues to speak out against violence and injustice. Kamate works to raise awareness about how local communities suffer while powerful groups exploit the country’s resources.

**Impact** – Kamate’s peaceful approach is unusual in a region where violence is common. He shows that change can come from non-violence and unity. His efforts help bring attention to the hidden struggles of Congolese people and challenge both local and global actors who benefit from the conflict. Kamate also highlights the importance of listening to local voices rather than imposing outside solutions.

**What’s next step** – Kamate encourages more spaces where young people can share their ideas and demands openly. He believes that if many people join together peacefully, they can create real change. His work aims to build hope and solidarity among communities affected by violence, inspiring others to act now rather than wait.

**One-sentence takeaway** – François Kamate’s peaceful activism in the DRC shows that even in difficult times, standing up together with hope can challenge injustice and inspire change.
articles.personality_title_fr: François Kamate lutte pacifiquement pour la justice en RDC malgré le conflit
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La République démocratique du Congo (RDC) est riche en ressources naturelles, mais connaît une violence, une corruption et une pauvreté durables. Ces problèmes rendent la vie difficile pour la population. De nombreux groupes armés se battent pour le contrôle des minerais précieux utilisés dans les téléphones et les voitures électriques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – François Kaserake Kamate, militant pour le climat et les droits humains dans l’est de la RDC, mène depuis 13 ans des mouvements pacifiques et des manifestations non violentes. Malgré des arrestations, des menaces et la perte de son emploi, il continue de dénoncer la violence et l’injustice. Kamate sensibilise sur les souffrances des communautés locales face à l’exploitation des ressources par des groupes puissants.

**Impact** – L’approche pacifique de Kamate est rare dans une région marquée par la violence. Il montre que le changement peut venir par la non-violence et l’unité. Ses actions attirent l’attention sur les luttes cachées du peuple congolais et défient les acteurs locaux et mondiaux qui profitent du conflit. Kamate souligne aussi l’importance d’écouter les voix locales plutôt que d’imposer des solutions extérieures.

**Prochaine étape** – Kamate encourage la création d’espaces où les jeunes peuvent exprimer librement leurs idées et demandes. Il croit que si beaucoup s’unissent pacifiquement, ils peuvent provoquer un vrai changement. Son travail vise à construire l’espoir et la solidarité parmi les communautés touchées par la violence, inspirant d’autres à agir dès maintenant.

**Une phrase clé** – L’activisme pacifique de François Kamate en RDC montre que même dans les moments difficiles, s’unir avec espoir peut défier l’injustice et inspirer le changement.
articles.personality_title_es: François Kamate lucha pacíficamente por la justicia en el Congo en medio del conflicto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La República Democrática del Congo (RDC) es rica en recursos naturales, pero sufre violencia, corrupción y pobreza que llevan años afectando a su gente. Muchos grupos armados pelean por controlar minerales valiosos usados en teléfonos y autos eléctricos.

**Qué pasó** – François Kaserake Kamate, activista por el clima y los derechos humanos del este de la RDC, ha liderado durante 13 años movimientos pacíficos y protestas no violentas. A pesar de arrestos, amenazas y perder su trabajo, sigue denunciando la violencia y la injusticia. Kamate busca dar voz a las comunidades locales que sufren mientras grupos poderosos explotan los recursos del país.

**Impacto** – El enfoque pacífico de Kamate es inusual en una zona marcada por la violencia. Él demuestra que el cambio puede venir a través de la no violencia y la unión. Sus esfuerzos llaman la atención sobre las luchas del pueblo congoleño y desafían a actores locales y globales que se benefician del conflicto. También destaca la importancia de escuchar las voces locales en lugar de imponer soluciones externas.

**Próximo paso** – Kamate impulsa la creación de espacios donde los jóvenes puedan expresar sus ideas y demandas libremente. Cree que si muchas personas se unen pacíficamente, podrán lograr un cambio real. Su trabajo busca construir esperanza y solidaridad en las comunidades afectadas por la violencia, inspirando a otros a actuar ahora.

**Frase clave** – El activismo pacífico de François Kamate en la RDC muestra que, incluso en tiempos difíciles, unirse con esperanza puede desafiar la injusticia e inspirar el cambio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a cracked, mineral-rich earth shaped like the Democratic Republic of the Congo, with glowing veins of precious minerals intertwined with fragile, intertwined hands rising peacefully from the soil, symbolizing non-violent resistance and hope amidst conflict and exploitation.
